data.title: Beta Bionics projects $131M-$136M 2026 revenue and raises gross margin outlook to 57.5%-59.5%
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Beta Bionics (BBNX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We're pleased to share with you all today our financial results for the first quarter as well as positive updates to our full year guidance for 2026." (President, CEO & Director Sean Saint)

  • "In Q1, the company continued to progress rapidly across our key initiatives, both commercially in terms of driving adoption of the iLet and expanding pharmacy channel access and developmentally in terms of advancing our Mint patch pump program and our bihormonal program." (President, CEO & Director Saint)

  • "We delivered $27.6 million in net sales, which grew 57% year-over-year." (President, CEO & Director Saint)

  • "The percentage of new patient starts that were reimbursed through the pharmacy channel grew to a high 30s percentage compared to a low 30s percentage in Q4 and a low 20s percentage in Q1 2025." (President, CEO & Director Saint)

  • "Our gross margin was 59.5%, expanding over 860 basis points year-over-year." (President, CEO & Director Saint)

  • "Our Q1 performance exceeded our expectations across the board." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Stephen Feider)

  • "New patient starts declined more than 10%, but less than 20% compared to Q4 2025, consistent with our expectations given typical seasonal demand patterns from Q4 to Q1." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

  • "We crossed that threshold in Q1, and we expect further gross margin expansion as our pharmacy installed base continues to grow." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

  • "We are building a business that we believe is uniquely positioned to succeed over the short, medium and long term." (President, CEO & Director Saint)

Outlook

  • "We now project total revenue for the year to be $131 million to $136 million, up from our prior guidance of $130 million to $135 million." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

  • "On pharmacy mix, we now expect 37% to 39% of our new patient starts to be reimbursed through the pharmacy channel versus our prior guidance of 36% to 38%." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

  • "On gross margin, we are raising our outlook to 57.5% to 59.5% for the full year versus our prior guidance of 55.5% to 57.5%." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

  • "We do not expect an uptick from Q1 to Q2." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

  • "To briefly comment on operating expenses, we expect year-over-year growth to accelerate for the remainder of the year compared to Q1." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

Financial Results

  • "Q1 gross margin was 59.5%, representing an increase of 52 basis points relative to the prior quarter and an increase of 864 basis points relative to the prior year." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

  • "Total operating expenses in the first quarter were $40.7 million." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

  • "As of March 31, 2026, we have approximately $240 million in cash, cash equivalents and short- and long-term investments." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

  • "We also benefited from a couple of onetime gross margin tailwinds in the quarter, including higher-than-planned iLet production and modest contribution from pharmacy iLet revenue." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

Q&A

  • Michael Kratky, Leerink Partners: "Is there any fundamental reason driving that assumption or anything you're seeing from a competitive standpoint that may be tempering expectations there?" CFO Feider: "In terms of competitive pressure that we're feeling as it relates to the pharmacy channel, none at all that's dampening guidance in any way."

  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: "Could you give us some perspective on what you're observing in the field as it relates to prescribing patterns?" President, CEO & Director Saint: "I don't think that the ADA guideline changes, while helpful, are really impacting prescribing patterns on a daily basis."

  • David Roman, Goldman Sachs: "Are you just seeing a higher customer acquisition cost as the market becomes more competitive?" CFO Feider: "Our sales and marketing costs in Q1 '26 are 75% of our revenue."

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street: "Help quantify some of the benefits you called out" on Q1 margin. CFO Feider: "I don't want to quantify specifically what that impact was, but it was relatively small but notable."

  • Frank Takkinen, Lake Street: "Any seasonal considerations" on cash burn. CFO Feider: "We burned about $25 million in Q1."

  • Jonathan Block, Stifel: asked about quarterly revenue cadence and "$31 million for 2Q '26"; CFO Feider: "I'm sorry, I'm not going to specifically comment on the number you shared in terms of Q2 guidance."

  • Felipe Lamar, Truist: asked why pharmacy economics should hold with more entrants. President, CEO & Director Saint: "The primary reason for the moment is that insulin pumps are a non-commoditized market."

  • Jeffrey Johnson, Baird: asked about rebates vs co-pay buy-down and tiering. President, CEO & Director Saint: "Currently, I believe we're at $25 or less per month."

  • Matthew O'Brien, Piper Sandler: asked if type 2 is driving growth and exposure without an indication. CFO Feider: "The fact that we cannot promote our product for type 2... That does hinder our growth, yes."

  • Jeffrey Cohen, Ladenburg: asked about cost-of-materials benefit. CFO Feider: "The primary driver... is simply just volume."

  • Ryan Schiller, Wolfe: asked about competitive changes. President, CEO & Director Saint: "No... IPOs don't really have any bearing whatsoever on the actual market dynamics as far as we're concerned."

Sentiment Analysis

  • "Are you just seeing a higher customer acquisition cost as the market becomes more competitive?" (David Roman, Goldman Sachs) and "help quantify" margin one-timers (Frank Takkinen, Lake Street) reflected a slightly negative, probing analyst tone focused on sustainability of growth, margins, and spend.

  • "Our Q1 performance exceeded our expectations across the board" and "none at all that's dampening guidance in any way" (CFO Feider) reflected slightly positive management tone in Q&A, while maintaining guarded answers on predictability ("this particular area isn't perfectly predictable") and one-time items ("I don't want to quantify").

  • Compared with Q4 2025, management language shifted from initial 2026 framing to explicit upward revisions, while analysts remained centered on pharmacy conversion durability, quarterly cadence, and profitability path.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 2026 emphasized guidance increases (revenue, pharmacy mix, and gross margin), whereas Q4 2025 introduced the initial 2026 ranges and highlighted seasonality headwinds from Q4-to-Q1.

  • Q1 2026 discussion added operational detail on pharmacy mechanics ("we do not expect an uptick from Q1 to Q2") and margin thresholds ("exceeds 3x" framework), while Q4 2025 focused more on disclosure changes ("we will no longer provide an exact quarterly new patient starts figure").

  • Analyst focus stayed consistent quarter-to-quarter on pharmacy conversion and sales force expansion, but Q1 2026 expanded into spend efficiency and competitive/customer acquisition dynamics, anchored by management’s "75% of our revenue" sales and marketing comment.

Risks and Concerns

  • "I'll briefly touch on our remediation efforts regarding the FDA warning letter we received in late January" and "we still have work to do in other areas to fully address the agency's concerns." (President, CEO & Director Saint)

  • "We don't expect those onetime tailwinds to repeat" on gross margin, alongside the note that pharmacy reimbursement is "not perfectly predictable" due to a "long sales cycle." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

  • Management flagged near-term uncertainty in cost and profitability optics, stating that current sales and marketing intensity "doesn't look like a perfectly economical sales and marketing model at this exact moment." (CFO, Treasurer & Secretary Feider)

Final Takeaway

Beta Bionics’ management highlighted Q1 2026 net sales of $27.6 million, a 59.5% gross margin, and rising pharmacy penetration in new patient starts, then raised 2026 guidance across revenue ($131 million to $136 million), pharmacy mix (37% to 39%), and gross margin (57.5% to 59.5%). Management also reiterated active remediation of the FDA warning letter, described Mint as targeting an "unconstrained commercial launch by the end of 2027," and positioned ongoing sales force and direct-to-consumer investments as building an installed base—particularly in pharmacy—intended to improve longer-term economics.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Calix forecasts 15% to 20% revenue growth for 2026 as it rolls out memory surcharges
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Calix (CALX) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "It was another incredible execution quarter for the Calix team. Record revenue with strong demand continuing into 2026 with customers." (CEO, President & Director Michael Weening)

  • "At the end of March, we completed the migration of all existing customers to the third generation of the Calix platform. Launching on Google Cloud, thereby enabling the expansion of our capabilities and the markets that we target." (CEO, President & Director Weening)

  • "In the first quarter of 2026, Calix delivered yet another quarter of record revenue of $280 million, marking a sequential increase of 3%, driven by continued strong demand for our platform." (Chief Financial Officer Cory Sindelar)

  • "Remaining performance obligations were $376 million, down 2% sequentially and up 11% year-over-year" and "Current RPOs in the first quarter were a record $157 million." (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)

  • "We also invested $171 million to buy back 3.3 million shares of our common stock at an average price of $51.34" and "the Board today authorized another $100 million to be added to this program." (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)

Outlook

  • "Our revenue guidance for the second quarter of 2026 is between $287 and $293 million" and "For the year, we expect revenue to grow between 15% and 20%." (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)

  • "This reflects continued robust demand trends and a modest benefit from recapturing a portion of the higher memory costs via a memory surcharge." (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)

  • "Our gross margin guidance for the second quarter of 2026 is between 54.25% and 57.25%." (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)

  • "For the year, we expect our non-GAAP gross margin to decline between 50 and 150 basis points." (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)

  • Change from the prior quarter’s commentary on 2026 growth: in Q4 2025, management discussed 2026 growth "somewhere in that range of 10% to 15%" (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar); in Q1 2026, management updated the full-year expectation to "between 15% and 20%" (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar).

Financial Results

  • "Non-GAAP gross margin was 57.2%, down 80 basis points sequentially" and "we generated free cash flow in the quarter of $7 million." (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)

  • "Our advanced purchasing had allowed us to avoid higher memory component costs during the first quarter. However, that advanced supply has run its course, and we now face market prices." (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)

  • "We are partnering with our customers to share in the higher memory costs, by initiating a surcharge, albeit it is a partial cost recovery and without adding gross profit." (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)

Q&A

  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: asked whether appliance gross margins recover in the back half as surcharges flow through and what prevents further margin downside if memory costs rise; Chief Financial Officer Sindelar: "our plan is to recover the costs" and "if there are further cost increases, we would adjust the surcharges accordingly" and "the effect of the surcharges for here to the end of the year, represent a 200 basis point headwind because you're adding a large amount of revenue at 0 points of margin through."

  • Samik Chatterjee, JPMorgan: asked how much of higher Q2 revenue and the higher 2026 growth range is demand vs. surcharges; Chief Financial Officer Sindelar: "The majority of the quarter-over-quarter increase is due to customer demand and a lesser portion is the surcharges" and "the majority of that increase in revenue is coming from increased demand."

  • Scott Searle, ROTH: asked if "dual cloud" cost goes away in 2H and how to model it; Chief Financial Officer Sindelar: "we've got all the customers migrated onto the new cloud" and "the dual cloud environment is done" and "the penalty that we incurred happened in the first quarter."

  • Scott Searle, ROTH: asked whether fiber availability or Starlink is changing customer rollout behavior; CEO, President & Director Weening: "I don't hear anyone saying I need to accelerate my rollout of fiber to compete" and "another competitive pressure is a good thing for Calix."

  • Christian Schwab, Craig-Hallum: asked if the move from 10%–15% to 15%–20% revenue growth is surcharges vs. accelerating demand; Chief Financial Officer Sindelar: "the effect of surcharges is going to move us up" and "we're also seeing some of the best demand that we've seen."

  • Christian Schwab, Craig-Hallum: asked BEAD peak year and revenue magnitude; Chief Financial Officer Sindelar: "you'll start to see this thing ramp more significantly in 2027, probably peaks in '28" and "it's potentially high tens of millions."

  • Karen on for George Notter, Wolfe Research: asked for Tier 1 cloud traction; Chief Financial Officer Sindelar: "None that we're willing to share."

  • Timothy Savageaux, Northland: asked whether FCC router rules impact Calix and whether conditional approval timing affects the business; Chief Financial Officer Sindelar: "we would expect to be receiving our conditional approval here soon" and "any existing product that's shipping is not at risk"; CEO, President & Director Weening: "Absolutely not" and "It's a nonevent."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to neutral, concentrated on gross-margin durability under memory inflation (surcharges and pass-through mechanics) and external variables (BEAD timing, FCC approvals, Starlink/fiber).

  • Management: positive in prepared remarks and firm in Q&A, repeatedly emphasizing execution and platform milestones, including "Record revenue" and "the dual cloud environment is done" (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar), and describing 2026 as "very bullish" after migration completion (CEO, President & Director Weening).

  • Versus last quarter: Q1 2026 tone shifted to more direct operational assertions (migration completed; dual-cloud ended) and a higher 2026 growth range, while last quarter carried more transition framing (migration "by the end of first quarter" and "some near-term impact" from "overlapping cloud costs").

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Platform transition: Q4 2025 framed migration as in-progress ("working to complete all customer migrations by the end of first quarter" (CEO, President & Director Weening)); Q1 2026 stated completion ("we completed the migration of all existing customers" (CEO, President & Director Weening)).

  • Guidance language: Q4 2025 described 2026 growth "somewhere in that range of 10% to 15%" (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar); Q1 2026 raised it to "between 15% and 20%" and tied part of the uplift to a "memory surcharge" (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar).

  • BEAD framing: Q4 2025 outlined a Calix opportunity size ("between $1 billion and $1.5 billion" (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)); Q1 2026 emphasized nearer-term revenue timing ("tens of millions of dollars forecast in the second half of '26" (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)) and a peak year view ("peaks in '28" (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)).

Risks and Concerns

  • "With demand/supply disconnect, so large related to memory components" the company flagged supply continuity and cost pressure; mitigation was "advanced purchasing" (now "run its course") and customer cost-sharing via "initiating a surcharge" with "partial cost recovery." (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)

  • Management also warned surcharges can pressure reported margins: "the effect of the surcharges ... represent a 200 basis point headwind" because the revenue carries "0 points of margin." (Chief Financial Officer Sindelar)

Final Takeaway

Calix management positioned Q1 2026 around a completed third-generation platform migration to Google Cloud, record quarterly revenue of $280 million, and a higher 2026 revenue growth outlook of 15% to 20%. Management emphasized that higher memory costs are now a primary swing factor, with customer surcharges intended to recover costs while also creating a margin headwind, and highlighted capital return through $171 million of buybacks plus a new $100 million authorization.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Hancock Whitney anticipates mid-single-digit 2026 loan growth while targeting 12%–12.5% CET1 by end of 2028
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Hancock Whitney Corporation (HWC) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "We are pleased to report a solid start to 2026. Our adjusted ROA was 1.43%, ROTCE was 14.64% and EPS was $1.52, all improved from prior quarter." (President, CEO & Director John Hairston)
  • "We are very excited to welcome 27 net new revenue producers to our strong banking team, and we expect to build on the momentum we have to generate meaningful balance sheet growth and profitability improvement over the rest of 2026." (President, CEO & Director John Hairston)
  • "Net interest margin expanded 7 basis points this quarter due to higher securities yields following our bond portfolio restructuring and lower cost of funds that outpaced the impacts of lower loan yields in this rate environment." (President, CEO & Director Hairston)
  • "This quarter, we continued to proactively return capital to shareholders through repurchasing 1.4 million shares of our common stock and increasing our quarterly cash dividend 11%, now standing at $0.50 per share." (President, CEO & Director Hairston)
  • "Adjusted for the net loss in the bond portfolio restructuring, net income for the first quarter was $125 million or $1.52 per share compared to $126 million or $1.49 per share in the fourth quarter." (Senior EVP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Michael Achary)
  • "We are, however, now assuming no rate cuts throughout 2026 with no significant impact to NII or our NIM." (Senior EVP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Achary)

Outlook

  • "As shown on Slide 20 of the investor deck, we remain confident in the guidance provided at the beginning of the year and have not made any changes this quarter." (Senior EVP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Achary)
  • "Our guidance of mid-single digits for the year for loan growth is unchanged." (President, CEO & Director Hairston)
  • "We have not changed our guidance on deposits as we still expect balances to be up low single digits from 2025 levels." (President, CEO & Director Hairston)
  • Change from last quarter’s rate-path assumption was explicit: in Q4, "We are assuming 2 25 basis point rate cuts in April and July of 2026." (Senior EVP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Achary, Q4 2025) In Q1, "we are... now assuming no rate cuts throughout 2026." (Senior EVP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Achary)

Financial Results

  • "PPNR for the company was down slightly from the prior quarter or about 1% to $173 million." (Senior EVP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Achary)
  • "Our NIM was up 7 basis points this quarter to 3.55%." (Senior EVP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Achary)
  • "Our overall cost of funds was down 8 basis points to 1.44%." (Senior EVP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Achary)
  • "Fee income adjusted for the net loss on the bond portfolio restructuring was essentially flat with last quarter, down only $1 million." (Senior EVP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Achary)
  • "For the fifth consecutive quarter, our criticized commercial loans improved, decreasing $13 million to $522 million." (Senior EVP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Achary)

Q&A

  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: loan growth trajectory and confidence into the back half of 2026; Senior EVP & COO D. Loper: "we're positioned to deliver the mid-single-digit full year growth" and "we've hired 27 net new bankers... with more coming in the second quarter." (Senior EVP, COO Loper)
  • Michael Rose, Raymond James: capital targets and CET1 over time; Senior EVP, CFO Achary: "for CET1, that companion number is probably between 12% and 12.5% or somewhere in that range" and those targets are "styled toward achievement in fourth quarter of '28." (Senior EVP, CFO Achary)
  • Matt Olney, Stephens: banker hiring mix and where they were hired; Senior EVP, COO Loper: "the majority of the bankers are hired" in Texas and "70% of these new bankers are in our business banking area" with "50 net new for '26." (Senior EVP, COO Loper)
  • Catherine Mealor, KBW: sustainability of NIM drivers and loan-yield outlook; Senior EVP, CFO Achary: "we're looking at the yield on the loan portfolio to largely remain kind of where it is right now" and NIM drivers were "the loan growth, the bond portfolio contribution and then lower cost of deposits." (Senior EVP, CFO Achary)
  • Casey Haire, Autonomous: size of unscheduled prepayments and assumptions; Senior EVP, COO Loper: "I don't have a specific number to give you" but it is "planned into our overall production reconciliation." (Senior EVP, COO Loper)
  • Brett Rabatin, StoneX: fee-income drivers and wealth-management sensitivity to markets; Senior EVP, COO Loper: "I do believe that it supports that 4% to 5% growth for the full year of '26" and "if we get some downward tilt to that market, it's going to put a little pressure on wealth management annuitized fee income." (Senior EVP, COO Loper)
  • Gary Tenner, D.A. Davidson: CD laddering strategy in a flatter rate outlook; Senior EVP, CFO Achary: "to the extent we can begin to kind of lengthen out some of those CD maturities" and highlighted "3.5% for 11 months." (Senior EVP, CFO Achary)

Sentiment Analysis

  • "investors are really looking forward to seeing pick up" loan growth, and analysts pressed for more specificity on growth drivers and paydowns. (Michael Rose, Raymond James: Question; Casey Haire, Autonomous: Question)
  • Management tone remained slightly positive and control-oriented, emphasizing unchanged guidance and balance-sheet levers, including "we remain confident" and "we're positioned to deliver the mid-single-digit full year growth." (Senior EVP, CFO Achary; Senior EVP, COO Loper)
  • Compared with last quarter, management’s rate messaging became more cautious on policy assumptions (Q4: "assuming 2... rate cuts" vs. Q1: "now assuming no rate cuts"), while confidence in overall guidance language stayed consistent (Q1: "have not made any changes"). (Senior EVP, CFO Achary)

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Q1 reiterated key operating themes from Q4—hiring and organic growth—while updating the interest-rate assumption (Q4: "2... rate cuts" vs. Q1: "no rate cuts") without changing broader guidance language. (Senior EVP, CFO Achary)
  • Analyst focus remained heavily concentrated on loan growth and its headwinds (Q4: production vs. paydowns; Q1: back-half growth cadence and unscheduled prepayments), with management repeatedly pointing to banker hiring and pipelines as the core mechanism. (Raymond James / Autonomous: Questions; Senior EVP, COO Loper: Answers)
  • Capital deployment discussion shifted from Q4’s repurchase authorization framing to Q1’s execution and longer-term targets (Q1: "repurchasing 1.4 million shares" and CET1 "between 12% and 12.5%" by end of 2028). (President, CEO & Director Hairston; Senior EVP, CFO Achary)

Risks and Concerns

  • "Deposits were down $198 million or 3% annualized due to seasonal public funds outflows." (President, CEO & Director Hairston)
  • On geopolitical and cost pressures, management emphasized limited visibility and monitoring: "it's probably early to tell" and "it's certainly something that we're watching and we're mindful of." (Senior EVP & Chief Credit Officer Christopher Ziluca)
  • On fee volatility, management flagged unpredictability in certain lines: "specialty income... tends to be somewhat unpredictable quarter-to-quarter." (Senior EVP, CFO & Principal Accounting Officer Achary)

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a stronger quarter sequentially, with profitability metrics improving and NIM expansion tied to the bond portfolio restructuring and lower funding costs. Leadership kept full-year guidance unchanged while explicitly shifting its 2026 rate assumption to "no rate cuts," and repeatedly framed banker hiring—"27 net new" with more planned—as the primary driver for loan growth acceleration into the back half of the year, alongside continued capital returns through buybacks and an 11% dividend increase.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Oil moves higher on Iran uncertainty; Trump extends ceasefire but maintains blockade
data.description:
Crude oil futures gained Tuesday, with the market unclear on whether Iran will send a delegation to Pakistan for talks with the U.S.; oil stayed higher after-hours as President Trump said the U.S. will extend its ceasefire with Iran and continue the blockade of the country's ports until its leaders present "a unified proposal."

Vice President Vance did not depart for Pakistan, where peace talks with Iran were supposed to resume, while Iranian officials have not publicly committed to participating in talks.

The U.S. Treasury Department also announced a series of new sanctions targeting Iran's oil-smuggling network, proxy groups, and weapons pipeline.

The White House hopes that depriving Iran of economic resources will pressure the country's leaders to return to peace talks and make concessions; the blockade alone could cost Iran well over $400M daily, including $276M in lost exports, mostly of crude oil and petrochemicals, according to one estimate.

Shipping traffic through the Strait of Hormuz, which normally handles ~20% of the world's oil and liquefied ⁠natural gas ​supplies, remained mostly shut Tuesday, ​with only three ships passing through the waterway in the past 24 hours, ​shipping data showed.

The oil supply disruption will only worsen this month as tanker traffic through the strait remains very low, Rystad Energy's chief oil analyst Paola Rodriguez-Masiu said in a note.

The Persian Gulf countries are expected to produce 14.3M bbl/day in April, 3M bbl/day below March levels and ~13M bbl/day below pre-war levels, Rodriguez-Masiu said.

Front-month Nymex crude (CL1:COM) for May delivery gained 2.8% to $92.13/bbl, and front-month Brent crude (CO1:COM) for June delivery jumped 3.1% to $98.48/bbl.

U.S. natural gas futures (NG1:COM) inched up in rangebound trading, with the market waiting out mild spring weather; the front-month Nymex May contract closed up 0.3% to $2.697/MMBtu.

ETF: (USO), (BNO), (UCO), (SCO), (USL), (DBO), (DRIP), (GUSH), (USOI), (UNG), (BOIL), (KOLD), (UNL), (FCG), (XLE)

data.title: Interactive Brokers signals capital return shift with dividend increase to $0.35 per year as Q1 net revenues reach record
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Interactive Brokers Group (IBKR) Q1 2026

Management View

  • Management said the quarter combined weaker broad markets with continued client growth and engagement: “We continue to see strong interest from both institutional and individual investors globally in opening and funding accounts. Client engagement remained healthy, trading activity increased and clients gradually took on more risk…” (Director of Investor Relations Nancy Stuebe, reading prepared remarks).

  • Management highlighted record client balances and product activity while noting market declines: “Growth in new accounts has driven higher clients' uninvested cash balances, which increased 35% year-over-year to a record $169 billion. Client equity rose 38% to $789 billion and was up 1% sequentially…” (Director of Investor Relations Stuebe, reading prepared remarks).

  • Management emphasized AI as an active product and efficiency investment across research and service workflows: “We have been incorporating AI across the organization… expanded international company coverage… continued enhancing our Ask IBKR tool… our AI-powered chatbot continues to improve… We are also applying AI to further automate processes across areas like onboarding, compliance and other operational areas.” (Director of Investor Relations Stuebe, reading prepared remarks).

  • Management tied profitability and capital allocation to an increased dividend: “Our pretax profit margin was 77%… the sixth consecutive quarter with margins above 70%… we revisited our allocation of capital and decided to increase the amount of dividend we paid to $0.35 a year.” (Director of Investor Relations Stuebe, reading prepared remarks).

  • The company described several product rollouts aimed at expanding addressable trading activity, including crypto, prediction markets, and overnight trading: “Overnight trading volumes nearly tripled year-over-year in the first quarter, increasing to 8.1 million trades from 2.8 million, and up from 6.2 million in the fourth quarter.” (Director of Investor Relations Stuebe, reading prepared remarks).

  • “We are pleased with our financial results this quarter as we again produced record net revenues and strong results in our key operating metrics.” (CFO Paul Brody).

Outlook

  • No formal revenue or EPS guidance was provided in the transcript; management instead framed earnings power around activity levels, expense discipline, and rate sensitivity.

  • Management presented interest-rate sensitivity as a key swing factor: “We estimate the effect of a 25 basis point decrease in the benchmark Fed funds rate to be an $80 million reduction in annual net interest income… [and] a 25 basis point decrease in all the relevant non-USD benchmark rates would reduce annual net interest income by $35 million.” (CFO Brody).

  • In Q&A, management characterized the SEC’s removal of the Pattern Day Trader rule as a potential engagement tailwind for its largely retail base: “It is an opportunity in the sense that majority of our accounts are individual accounts… Many of these individual accounts are smaller accounts, and they will be able to trade frequently.” (President and CEO Milan Galik).

Financial Results

  • Commissions and interest income were positioned as major drivers: “Commissions rose 19% versus last year's first quarter, reaching over $600 million for the first time… Net interest income rose 17% year-on-year to $904 million.” (CFO Brody).

  • The company cited transactional efficiency and high margins: “Execution and clearing costs were 13% of commission revenues in the first quarter for a gross transactional profit margin of 87%.” (CFO Brody).

  • Expense items highlighted included compensation and advertising: “Compensation and benefits expense was $167 million for the quarter… Our headcount at March 31 was 3,232… G&A expenses were $68 million, up from the year ago quarter, mainly on expansion of advertising.” (CFO Brody).

  • Balance-sheet items emphasized capital strength and no leverage: “Our total assets were… $219 billion… We continue to have no long-term debt… firm equity up 23% to $21.3 billion.” (CFO Brody).

Q&A

  • Patrick Moley, Piper Sandler: asked about the SEC eliminating the Pattern Day Trader rule and whether it creates a structural growth opportunity; President and CEO Galik said, “We welcome the change… [it] will broaden the retail access, increase the trading frequency and engagement,” while adding it could accelerate both gains and losses for less disciplined traders.

  • James Yaro, Goldman Sachs: pressed on scaling marketing spend; Founder and Chairman Thomas Peterffy responded, “We are hell bent on trying to increase our marketing spend, but we are also very strict about getting the required minimum return on every additional marketing dollar.”

  • James Yaro, Goldman Sachs: asked about AI-enabled cash optimization tools at banks/brokers and implications for deposit pricing; Founder and Chairman Peterffy said, “We're not happy about these tools… if these tools force other brokers to do the same, then we're going to have more competition,” while President and CEO Galik added, “it isn't that much that AI needs to do here… [it’s] the bank's decision of how much… they want the client to enjoy.”

  • Benjamin Budish, Barclays: asked for intra-month margin balance detail; Founder and Chairman Peterffy pointed to disclosed monthly reporting and said, “So our margin loans are precisely at the end of the quarter, $86.6 billion.” Budish also asked about prediction markets adoption; Peterffy said, “ForecastEx is receiving more and more inquiries… and now more and more of them are curious and are considering becoming members.”

  • Brennan Hawken, BMO: requested a currency breakdown of non-USD rate-sensitive balances; CFO Brody said, “we don't really get into it at that granular level,” and added rate impacts are “roughly symmetrical” up vs. down with minor exceptions near zero/negative.

  • Christopher Allen, KBW: asked early traction on crypto transfers; President and CEO Galik said, “We do see amounts coming in,” and described the EEA crypto launch as a “soft release.” Allen also asked what’s needed to deepen crypto penetration; Galik said geographic expansion (including Singapore) and “staking” were key next steps.

  • Karim Assef, Bank of America: asked about Coinbase Derivatives economics and demand; President and CEO Galik said the offering targets retail sizing and that “perpetual cryptocurrency futures… command very, very significant volumes,” adding, “It's not a very large number of accounts yet, but the ones that are trading it are trading it in big numbers.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts’ tone was slightly positive to neutral, focused on structural catalysts (Pattern Day Trader rule change), spend scaling, and crypto/prediction-market monetization; several questions pressed for more granularity (marketing trends, currency mix, margin balances).

  • Management tone was positive in prepared remarks (records, margins, product breadth) and more mixed in Q&A: supportive on regulatory change (“We welcome the change,” (President and CEO Galik)) while candid on competitive pressure in cash yields (“We're not happy… we're going to have more competition,” (Founder and Chairman Peterffy)).

  • Versus last quarter, more Q&A time centered on near-term industry/regulatory developments (Pattern Day Trader rule; AI cash tools) rather than license/charter progress, with management still emphasizing discipline and long-run expansion.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Capital return messaging shifted from Q4’s broad confidence narrative to an explicit action in Q1: Q1 management said it “decided to increase” the dividend to $0.35 per year, while Q4 emphasized record-year results and ongoing investment without a dividend change.

  • Prediction markets discussion moved from Q4’s regulatory uncertainty around sports contracts and specific volume metrics (e.g., “ForecastEx traded 286 million pairs,” in Q4) to Q1 commentary centered on increasing inbound institutional interest (“receiving more and more inquiries,” (Founder and Chairman Peterffy)) and election-related tools.

  • Q1 introduced the Pattern Day Trader rule change as a new potential industry tailwind, a topic absent from Q4, while Q4’s bank charter and potential European license discussion did not recur in Q1.

Risks and Concerns

  • Rate sensitivity remained a disclosed earnings risk: “a 25 basis point decrease… [is] an $80 million reduction in annual net interest income.” (CFO Brody).

  • Management flagged competitive risk if the industry improves cash yields through new tools: “if these tools force other brokers to do the same, then we're going to have more competition.” (Founder and Chairman Peterffy).

  • Management described volatility’s mixed impact on growth dynamics: “as the volatility increases, that may discourage newcomers… but that gets offset by increase in the DARTs.” (President and CEO Galik).

Final Takeaway

Interactive Brokers’ management framed Q1 2026 around record operating metrics and a sustained high margin profile, while highlighting product expansion (AI tooling, prediction markets, crypto, and growing overnight trading) and a tangible capital allocation change via a higher $0.35-per-year dividend. In Q&A, executives pointed to the elimination of the Pattern Day Trader rule as an account-engagement opportunity, reiterated marketing-return discipline, and underscored that net interest income remains meaningfully sensitive to benchmark rate moves.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: NEXGEL expects pro forma annual revenue of roughly $35M following Celularity wound segment acquisition
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: NEXGEL (NXGL) Q4 2025

Management View

  • "On today's call, I will discuss our announcement this morning regarding the closing of our transaction to acquire Celularity's degenerative wound segment." (CEO, President & Director Adam Levy)

  • "This is a transformational step forward for our company, marking our evolution into a more scalable, diversified medical technology business, tripling our revenue run rate and immediately contributing to profitability." (CEO, President & Director Levy)

  • "Their lead investment of $5.5 million not only strengthened the financing structure of the transaction, but also aligns us with a partner that enhances our capabilities across all of the aforementioned verticals." (CEO, President & Director Levy) "The financing was done through convertible notes at a $0.60 conversion price and 50% warrant coverage with a strike price of $0.80." (CEO, President & Director Levy)

  • "We are excited to announce the formation of a new division, BioNX Surgical, a dedicated division focused on advanced biomaterials for tendon repair, soft tissue reconstruction, bone regeneration and of course, wound care." (CEO, President & Director Levy) "The acquired portfolio includes 6 established regenerative biomaterial products." (CEO, President & Director Levy)

  • "On a pro forma basis, we expect it to approximately triple our annual revenue to roughly $35 million and be immediately accretive to profitability upon closing." (CEO, President & Director Levy)

  • No CFO remarks were included in this Q4 2025 transcript.

Outlook

  • "Our focus is now on execution and successfully integrating these assets and driving commercial growth." (CEO, President & Director Levy)

  • "The company will be -- on an EBITDA basis for certain will be profitable." (CEO, President & Director Levy) "This is a seasonal product line. So Q1 is generally the weakest." (CEO, President & Director Levy)

  • "We have a fixed overhead that's pretty easy to quantify. And so it's going to be probably around the $6 million to $6.5 million range." (CEO, President & Director Levy)

  • "Q4 was surprisingly light for us." (CEO, President & Director Levy) "We're seeing a return to normality in Q1." (CEO, President & Director Levy)

Financial Results

  • "On a pro forma basis, we expect it to approximately triple our annual revenue to roughly $35 million." (CEO, President & Director Levy)

  • "In the case of this company doing the same business that they did last year, our models, if we did $22 million to $23 million, shows about $4 million to $4.5 million of EBITDA." (CEO, President & Director Levy)

  • "We expect to get a contribution margin of roughly 52%." (CEO, President & Director Levy) "Contribution margin is essentially cost of goods plus commissions and sales commissions and direct sales costs." (CEO, President & Director Levy)

Q&A

  • Nazibur Rahman, Maxim Group LLC, Research Division: asked for clarification on the cash, assumed liabilities, and how the $5.5 million Sequence investment connected to total consideration; CEO Levy: "we paid $5.3 million at closing to Celularity" and "assumed and are going to pay out the $2.9 million that is owed to the sales reps in back commissions."

  • Nazibur Rahman, Maxim Group LLC, Research Division: asked for convertible note terms; CEO Levy: "the note is an 18-month note" and "It pays a 10% coupon."

  • Nazibur Rahman, Maxim Group LLC, Research Division: asked about integration timing and costs; CEO Levy: "No." and "most of the operations kind of move over seamlessly."

  • Nazibur Rahman, Maxim Group LLC, Research Division: asked about 2026 margins/EBITDA; CEO Levy: "we expect to get a contribution margin of roughly 52%" and provided a model of "$22 million to $23 million" implying "about $4 million to $4.5 million of EBITDA" for the acquired business case.

  • Nazibur Rahman, Maxim Group LLC, Research Division: asked for early Q1 color after a light Q4; CEO Levy: "We're not going to see a drop off, especially in consumer in Q1."

  • Mike Andrews (listed as Unknown Analyst): asked where NexGelRx fits; CEO Levy: "It doesn't." and described it as "a separate spinout that NEXGEL owns 20% of."

  • Brett Derekson (listed as Unknown Analyst): asked about dilution from converts/warrants; CEO Levy: "for about every $1 million, there's 2.4 million shares" and "you're somewhere in the 30 million share area."

  • David Blocker (listed as Unknown Analyst): asked for 2–3 year revenue model; CEO Levy: "This was a business that had scaled all the way up to $50 million as recently as 2024" and "we hope to get back to that level and beyond."

  • Ves Mahallov (listed as Unknown Analyst): asked about royalties/IP; CEO Levy: "We have a 5% royalty to Celularity on the SPARK project" plus "3%" on ORCHID and "1%" on FUSE, and "they sunset, I think, after 7 years."

  • Ves Mahallov (listed as Unknown Analyst): pressed on consolidated profitability timing; CEO Levy: "on an EBITDA basis for certain will be profitable" and later added, "on a rolling 12-month basis" the company "should be both EBITDA and operating -- EBITDA profitable."

  • Robert Gotlieb (listed as Unknown Analyst): asked when acquired products start selling under NEXGEL; CEO Levy: "Yesterday," and "as of Monday, all sales ... will begin to generate revenue."

  • Robert Gotlieb (listed as Unknown Analyst): asked about repay vs convert outcomes for the note; CEO Levy: "if we do well... it would certainly convert" and warned, "it's convertible notes... it can turn ugly," while stating "we have a very high confidence level here."

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts: slightly negative to pressing on structure, dilution, and profitability timing, including repeated clarification requests such as "could you clarify all the terms" (Nazibur Rahman, Maxim Group LLC, Research Division) and a direct profitability framing: "will this be a profitable company on an operating margin basis or at least an EBITDA basis" (Ves Mahallov, listed as Unknown Analyst).

  • Management: slightly positive and defensive in places, combining confidence with risk language, including "we have a very high confidence level here" (CEO, President & Director Levy) alongside "I'm not going to sugarcoat it" about convert risk (CEO, President & Director Levy).

  • Versus the prior quarter, the current call leaned more on transaction execution and financing mechanics, while Q3 2025 included conventional operating metrics and guidance framing such as "I expect full year 2025 revenues of between $12 million and $12.5 million" (CEO, President & Director Levy, Q3 2025).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Strategic focus shifted from Q3 2025 operating updates (contract manufacturing, consumer brands, and partner rollout cadence) to Q4 2025 M&A integration and a new division, with CEO Levy calling BioNX Surgical "a transformational step forward" (Q4 2025).

  • Guidance language shifted from Q3 2025 standalone-company targets ("full year 2025 revenues of between $12 million and $12.5 million") (CEO, President & Director Levy, Q3 2025) to Q4 2025 pro forma scale claims ("roughly $35 million") and profitability assertions tied to the acquisition (CEO, President & Director Levy, Q4 2025).

  • Analyst focus moved from Q3 2025 logistics delays and a partner program uncertainty to Q4 2025 financing, dilution math, and profitability timing, including extended questioning on how "52% contribution margin" maps into EBITDA (Ves Mahallov, listed as Unknown Analyst).

Risks and Concerns

  • "It's a little confusing" was management’s own characterization of the multi-party financing flow (CEO, President & Director Levy), and the call included explicit caution: "it's convertible notes... it can turn ugly" if performance fails to support conversion (CEO, President & Director Levy).

  • "Q4 was surprisingly light for us" and "Some of the new products didn't really do what we thought they were going to do" framed near-term consumer volatility risk (CEO, President & Director Levy), with mitigation described as "we're seeing a nice recovery in Q1" (CEO, President & Director Levy).

Final Takeaway

Management centered the quarter on the Celularity degenerative wound segment acquisition and the Sequence LifeScience partnership, describing the deal as creating BioNX Surgical, expanding to six commercial products plus three 510(k) pipeline devices, and projecting pro forma annual revenue of roughly $35 million with immediate profitability accretion. In Q&A, investors pressed on the financing’s mechanics, dilution, and timing of consolidated profitability, while management emphasized seamless operational transition, seasonal revenue dynamics, and an expectation of EBITDA profitability as the acquired portfolio scales and a hospital-focused sales force is rebuilt.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Tractor Supply outlines fresh pet expansion to 700 stores by year-end while reaffirming 1% to 3% comp targets
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Tractor Supply Company (TSCO) Q1 2026

Management View

  • “The retail environment remains cautious but stable with spending focused on needs and small indulgences with some evidence of trip consolidation,” and “within farm and ranch, the broader market has slowed, though we continue to gain share,” including “one of our best share performances in Q1,” according to “Harry Lawton (President, CEO & Director)”.
  • Lawton said Q1 demand “played out in distinct phases, driven by weather timing,” but “overall, weather was neutral to our performance in the quarter,” while calling out that “in pet, the category remains pressured” and “our performance is below our expectations.”
  • “Kurt Barton (Executive VP, CFO & Treasurer)” said “our net sales performance was modestly below our expectations for the quarter,” as “new store sales outperformed expectations” but “comp store sales performance [was] below our expectations,” adding that “softness in companion animal… represented just over 100 basis point drag on our comparable store sales.”
  • “Seth Estep (Executive VP & Chief Merchandising Officer)” detailed a companion animal reset, saying the plan is “centered on 4 key areas: first, assortment transformation; second, exclusive brand innovation; third, digital capabilities; and fourth, customer engagement,” including fresh and frozen pet expansion “from approximately 80 stores today to more than 250 stores by the end of May, with a path to 700 stores by year-end.”

Outlook

  • Barton said, “We are reaffirming our full year 2026 guidance,” and “continue to target comp sales growth in the range of 1% to 3% for each of the remaining quarters,” while emphasizing, “we manage the business on the halves and not the quarter.”
  • On intra-year cadence, Barton said, “we expect gross margin to strengthen in the second half as comparisons ease and benefits from our new distribution center begin to flow through,” and added, “our 11th distribution center remains on schedule with shipping expected to begin in early Q4.”
  • On costs and assumptions, Barton said, “the current environment remains fluid,” and “we are managing the business based on what we know today and have not assumed any incremental benefit from refunds in our outlook.”

Financial Results

  • Lawton reported, “Net sales increased 3.6% to $3.59 billion,” “diluted earnings per share were $0.31,” and “comparable store sales increased 0.5%, with average ticket up 1.6% and transactions down 1%,” alongside “a record 40 Tractor Supply stores” opened in the quarter.
  • Barton reported profitability and expense metrics, saying “gross margin was 36.2%, flat to prior year,” and “SG&A increased 6.1% to $1.07 billion and as a percent of sales was 29.7%, an increase of 70 basis points,” attributing deleverage to “fixed cost deleverage,” “continued investment in strategic initiatives,” and “an accelerated new store opening cadence.”

Q&A

  • Peter Keith, Piper Sandler: “Is that category getting worse?” Hal Lawton: “we view our share performance in pet to be stable… for the last 4 or 5 quarters… albeit it’s below our expectations,” and “our plan and forecast assumes that we’ll have continued pressure for some time in that category and then we’ll kind of see gradual improvement.”
  • Michael Lasser, UBS: “how long do you think it will take to effectuate an improvement… and… 2% to 3% comp growth… what does that do” longer-term? Lawton: “our plan for the balance of this year assumes continued pressure,” and “we certainly don’t see it as a structurally low growth business.”
  • Zachary Beeck, Baird: macro since “Iran conflict” and oil sensitivity? Lawton: “Our customer remains very stable,” and on fuel, “we’ve updated our internal numbers… very conservative… and that’s incorporated in our overall guidance.”
  • Katharine McShane, Goldman Sachs: softer new customer acquisition and localization performance? Lawton: “our new customer growth is… predominantly relying on new stores,” and “200 stores of localization are outperforming the rest of the Fusion store base.”
  • Christopher Horvers, JPMorgan: structural vs mix vs online in pet, and cat under-index? Lawton: “we’re about 80% dog, 20% cat versus the market that’s 60-40,” and “we are seeing performance improvement in the stores that we expand our cat in.”
  • Spencer Hanus, Wolfe: cannibalization and dog population outlook? Kurt Barton: “Cannibalization continues to be modest… consistent with the last few years,” and Seth Estep: “we’re not currently assuming really any changes in the trend.”
  • Simeon Gutman, Morgan Stanley: companion animal comp embedded in guidance; what’s better in Q2? Barton: “we expected flat to slightly positive comps in companion animal… [now] likely to be at a flat or slight negative comp throughout this year,” and Estep said Q2 strength is showing in “seasonal business… live goods… Zero Turn lineup… and also like digital continues to be incredibly strong.”
  • Charles Grom, Gordon Haskett: updates on Neighbor’s Club, Garden Centers, Final Mile; cat mix/pricing? Lawton: “Neighbor’s Club continues to perform very well,” “We are over 1,000 stores now collectively with garden centers and/or live good tents,” and “nothing… I would call out from a mix difference between cat and dog”; Colin Yankee added Final Mile “delivery volume was up double digits” and called it “a massive enabler.”
  • Seth Sigman, Barclays: pricing, elasticity, commodity inflation? Estep: “EDLP is our true north,” and “we’re in a position to continue to operate within… inflation… 1% to 2%.”
  • Scot Ciccarelli, Truist: can transactions turn positive if pet negative; was C.U.E. above/below average? Barton: “the large animal… feed category… [is] the biggest traffic driver,” and “we can comp positive… even if there’s some softness… in… companion animal.”
  • Steven Forbes, Guggenheim: rural migration and wallet share confidence? Lawton: “the more rural the store, the stronger the comp is,” and on pet share, “we are gaining share in dog food by itself, in cat food by itself… [but] it mixes to a flat share on food in total.”
  • Charles Cerankosky, Northcoast: Q2 weather setup and mix? Lawton: “we’re in the midst of that seasonal ramp right now,” citing strength in “live goods,” “sprayers and chemicals,” and “riding lawn mowers.”

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were slightly negative, repeatedly pressing on companion animal durability and the long-term comp algorithm, including “Is that category getting worse?” and “how long do you think it will take.”
  • Management was slightly positive but acknowledged pressure, pairing caution (“our plan… assumes continued pressure”) with confidence signals such as “we remain confident in our ability to deliver on our full year expectations” (CFO Barton).
  • Versus Q4, the tone shifted from Q4’s emphasis that results were “below our expectations” and “transitory” promotional pressures to Q1’s heavier focus on pet structural headwinds and mitigation execution, while still “reaffirming our full year outlook” (CEO Lawton).

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • In Q1, management highlighted “softness in companion animal” as the primary comp drag and laid out specific operating actions (fresh/frozen rollout, cat expansion, Rx/services), whereas Q4 commentary centered on holiday/promotional dynamics and storm-lap impacts as key drivers.
  • Digital commentary strengthened sequentially, with Q1 describing “strong double-digit growth” and Final Mile supporting “20% plus digital growth” (CEO Lawton), compared with Q4’s “high single-digit growth.”
  • Guidance language was consistent quarter to quarter, with Q1 stating “reaffirming our full year 2026 guidance,” aligning with Q4’s initial full-year 2026 framework and its repeated reminder that results are managed “in halves rather than the quarters.”

Risks and Concerns

  • Barton said “tariff costs” continued to pressure margins, though “pressure [is] largely contained and mitigated,” and added, “the current environment remains fluid.”
  • Lawton flagged consumer behavior headwinds: “trip consolidation,” refund dollars being used “more cautiously,” and that “in pet, the category remains pressured,” while management’s mitigation centered on “clear and decisive actions… expanding our fresh pet offering, increasing cat assortment and enhancing our services and Rx capabilities.”

Final Takeaway

Management framed Q1 as a needs-based, share-gaining quarter with modestly softer-than-expected comps driven by companion animal, while keeping full-year targets intact. The company emphasized near-term sequential comp improvement with seasonal ramp, and positioned the most concrete self-help as accelerating pet assortment changes (fresh/frozen to 700 stores by year-end) and scaling operational enablers like Final Mile and localization to support traffic, conversion, and cost-to-serve over the balance of 2026.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Manhattan Associates raises 2026 outlook to $1.147B-$1.157B revenue and $5.29-$5.37 EPS as RPO targets $2.62B-$2.68B
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Manhattan Associates (MANH) Q1 2026

Management View

  • "Manhattan is off to a strong start to 2026, navigating a volatile global macro, reporting record better-than-expected results," said (President, CEO & Director Eric Clark). "Our Q1 revenue growth accelerated, highlighted by 24% growth in cloud revenue," and "RPO increasing 24% to $2.35 billion."
  • "New customer bookings remained strong as over 55% of new cloud bookings were generated from net new logos" and "our win rate metric continues to be consistently above 70%," said (President, CEO & Director Clark), adding that the "largest Q1 deal" was "influenced by Google Cloud Marketplace."
  • "Our active agent pilot program is off to a better-than-expected start," said (President, CEO & Director Clark), describing deployment as "deployed in minutes, not months" and citing early customer outcomes including "exceptions requiring triage were reduced by up to 75%" at a food distribution customer and "increased line shipped by over 30%" for an industrial distribution customer.
  • "We closed a substantial new logo order management deal with one of the world's largest retailers," said (President, CEO & Director Clark), calling it "our largest ever OMS bookings deal." He also said the company "closed a large unified warehouse and transportation deal at a major retailer in Q1" tied to "running a single application for distribution and logistics."
  • "For the quarter, total revenue was $282 million," and "cloud revenue increased 24% to $117 million," said (CFO, Chief Accounting Officer and Treasurer Linda Pinne). "We ended Q1 with RPO of $2.35 billion, up 24% compared to the prior year and 5% sequentially."

Outlook

  • "Given our strong Q1 performance, we are raising our full year total revenue, operating margin and EPS outlook," said (CFO Pinne), while emphasizing, "the macro environment remains volatile" and "clarity from external variables remains limited."
  • "For total revenue, we expect $1.147 billion to $1.157 billion" and "for Q2, we continue to target total revenue of $285 million to $289 million," said (CFO Pinne).
  • "For adjusted operating margin, we are increasing the midpoint to 35%, up from our prior midpoint of 34.75%," said (CFO Pinne). "Our full year adjusted EPS range is increasing to $5.29 to $5.37," and "we are targeting Q2 EPS of $1.30."
  • "We continue to target RPO of $2.62 billion to $2.68 billion," said (CFO Pinne), adding that bookings can be "nonlinear" given large-deal timing.

Financial Results

  • "Q1 adjusted operating profit was $91 million with an operating margin of 32.4%," said (CFO Pinne), attributing outperformance to "strong cloud revenue growth, which offset some of the increased go-to-market investments we highlighted in Q4."
  • "We delivered better-than-expected adjusted earnings per share of $1.24" and "GAAP EPS was $0.82," said (CFO Pinne). She added GAAP EPS "was adversely impacted by higher-than-expected tax expense due to a decrease of stock-based compensation benefits."
  • "Q1 operating cash flow increased 12% to $84 million," said (CFO Pinne). "We ended the quarter with $226 million in cash and $0 debt," and "invested $150 million in share repurchases in the quarter" with "$350 million remaining" on the repurchase authority.

Q&A

  • Terrell Tillman, Truist Securities, Inc., Research Division: Asked how pilots convert into scaled revenue and how often customers run agents autonomously; (President, CEO & Director Clark) said, "our primary go-to-market is through a 90-day pilot" that is "a paid pilot," and management expects a "bigger impact" in 2027 than 2026; on autonomy, he said "most of our agents are designed to be autonomous if they so choose."
  • Brian Peterson, Raymond James & Associates, Inc., Research Division: Asked what drove strong RPO/new logo mix and about fixed services; (President, CEO & Director Clark) said deal volume rose across types and geographies, with "the largest 2 deals" coming from "Europe and APAC," and added new-logo strength has been elevated for "the past 5 quarters."
  • George Michael Kurosawa, Citigroup Inc., Research Division: Asked about cloud upside sustainability and forward-deployed engineers; (CFO Pinne) cited "onetime cloud overage fees" and "lower than modeled churn" while keeping "a conservative position" for Q2-Q4, and (President, CEO & Director Clark) said the company added "about 120 headcount" to services with "another roughly 70" pending/open.
  • Guy Hardwick, Barclays Bank PLC, Research Division: Asked pilot uplift and fixed-fee share of services; (President, CEO & Director Clark) said pricing "varies" and is not disclosed, that margins are modeled so "the margin remains consistent," and on fixed-fee mix, "I don't have an answer for that."
  • Lachlan Brown, Rothschild & Co Redburn, Research Division: Asked agent consumption and why guidance range moved more at the low end; (President, CEO & Director Clark) said consumption is used to determine pricing and results were "coming in as we expected," while (CFO Pinne) said, "we took our beat from Q1" but kept out-quarter parameters unchanged due to macro volatility.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analyst tone was slightly positive, with repeated focus on RPO strength, cloud upside sustainability, and monetization of Active Agents, plus direct probing on pricing, autonomy, and services durability.
  • Management tone was slightly positive in prepared remarks and more measured in Q&A, using cautionary language such as "we continue to take a conservative approach" (President, CEO & Director Clark) and "keeping those metrics in line" (CFO Pinne) despite the raised full-year outlook.
  • Compared with last quarter, management commentary showed continued confidence in demand and win rates, with more Q1-specific proof points on Agentic deployments and a clearer near-term conversion path from pilots to subscriptions.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Leadership change was a key shift: (President, CEO & Director Clark) said, "we have a new CFO," thanking Dennis Story and introducing Linda Pinne.
  • Guidance language tightened upward: in Q1, (CFO Pinne) said, "we are raising our full year total revenue, operating margin and EPS outlook," whereas Q4 management introduced the 2026 baseline outlook and emphasized investments.
  • AI narrative moved from launch framing in Q4 (commercial availability and 90-day pilots) to early customer outcomes and operational deployment examples in Q1, with (President, CEO & Director Clark) highlighting dozens of customers in pilots/paying usage and specific performance improvements.

Risks and Concerns

  • "The macro environment remains volatile" and "FX volatility continues to impact us," said (CFO Pinne), who noted Q1 FX was "an approximate $5 million headwind" to sequential RPO growth.
  • Cloud revenue drivers included items management flagged as non-recurring: "some onetime cloud overage fees that would not be recurring," said (CFO Pinne), alongside "lower than modeled churn" in renewals.

Final Takeaway

Management described Q1 as a better-than-expected start to 2026 with higher cloud growth, expanding RPO, and raised full-year revenue, margin, and EPS outlook, while repeatedly emphasizing macro volatility and maintaining conservative assumptions for Q2-Q4. Leadership highlighted accelerated go-to-market execution, sustained win rates, and early traction and measurable customer outcomes from Active Agents, positioning pilot-to-subscription conversions as the near-term monetization mechanism and pointing to a larger expected AI contribution in 2027.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Congressional group set to file legislation for year-round sales of E15 gasoline across U.S.
data.description:
A group of Republican congressional lawmakers is poised to file long-awaited legislation that would allow for year-round, nationwide sales of higher-ethanol gasoline, Argus reported Tuesday.

The proposal reportedly would limit exemptions from annual biofuel blending mandates to companies that process no more than 75K bbl/day of total refining capacity across all their facilities and would allow for exemptions for refineries that are at risk of closure.

The group plans to file the legislation on Wednesday, following years of efforts by the agriculture industry to pass legislation allowing year-round sales of E15 gasoline nationwide; legislation has failed consistently as much of the oil industry has historically opposed the attempts, since refiners are burdened with expenses from biofuel blending.

The latest proposal, like earlier drafts, would allow year-round sales of gasoline blends containing 15% ethanol, which farm groups have favored as a way to help corn growers and drivers alike; regulators over the years have often taken emergency action to waive summertime E15 limits that prevent its sale in much of the U.S., but permanent access requires legislation.

Potentially relevant stocks include Valero Energy (VLO), Marathon Petroleum (MPC), Phillips 66 (PSX), HF Sinclair (DINO), PBF Energy (PBF), Delek US (DK), Archer Daniels Midland (ADM), Bunge (BG), Green Plains (GPRE), Gevo (GEVO), Clean Energy Fuels (CLNE), REX American Resources (REX), Darling Ingredients (DAR), FutureFuel (FF).

ETF: (CRAK)

data.title: JFK landing scare follows Nashville near-miss, LaGuardia fatal crash
data.description:
Two recent near-collision incidents at major U.S. airports are intensifying concerns over air traffic control procedures, runway operations and the growing number of close calls involving commercial aircraft.

At New York’s John F. Kennedy International Airport on Monday afternoon, two passenger jets were forced to abort their landings after onboard anti-collision systems warned of a developing conflict during simultaneous approaches to parallel runways. American Airlines (AAL) Flight 4464, arriving from Indianapolis under the call sign “Brickyard,” drifted off its intended approach path and moved toward Air Canada (ACDVF) Flight 554, arriving from Toronto as “Jazz 554.”

Air traffic control audio captured collision alarms sounding in both the tower and cockpits as controllers ordered evasive action. According to flight tracking data, the aircraft came within roughly 350 feet (106.68 m) vertically and 0.62 miles (1 km) horizontally before both crews executed go-arounds and later landed safely. The Federal Aviation Administration said it is investigating the event.

The New York incident follows another serious close call in April over Nashville International Airport, where two Southwest Airlines (LUV) Boeing 737s came within seconds of colliding during parallel-runway operations. In that case, one aircraft had initiated a routine go-around, but subsequent controller instructions reportedly directed the climbing jet into the path of another Southwest flight on final approach.

Both Nashville crews received urgent Resolution Advisories from their Traffic Collision Avoidance Systems, prompting immediate maneuvers to restore safe spacing. The FAA classified the episode as a loss of separation and opened a formal probe.

Though no injuries were reported in either case, the two incidents share striking similarities: parallel-runway operations, aborted landings or go-arounds, controller intervention and last-second reliance on cockpit warning systems to prevent disaster.

What investors should know

For regulators and investors, the episodes highlight mounting pressure on the nation’s aviation infrastructure. Airlines continue to benefit from strong travel demand, but repeated safety scares can raise operational costs, invite tighter oversight and strain already thin controller staffing levels. Aircraft makers, airlines and airport operators all have an interest in restoring confidence that the system can safely handle rising traffic volumes.

The JFK event also comes weeks after a fatal accident at LaGuardia Airport involving a Jazz Aviation aircraft operating for Air Canada, adding to public focus on regional carrier operations in congested Northeast airspace.

Taken together, the Nashville and JFK incidents suggest the aviation system’s final line of defense is increasingly cockpit technology and fast pilot reactions.
